result:
All fixes are validated end-to-end against a real production build of this exact source. Here's the summary:

**Root cause of the traffic collapse:** this SaaS pivoted from "InboxZap" (email cleanup) to "Lumina" (AI meeting assistant), and the migration broke SEO in three ways:

1. **Brand/content mismatch** — `app/layout.tsx` still shipped InboxZap title/description/OG tags sitewide while every other signal (nav, content, JSON-LD elsewhere) said Lumina. Fixed: sitewide metadata, canonical, OG/Twitter, and `Organization` JSON-LD now consistently say Lumina; added page-level metadata to the homepage too.
2. **Blog completely broken** — 12 real, already-indexed articles (`/blog/post-1`…`post-12`, ~13k combined GSC impressions) had **no route in the app router at all**; the only blog code was a client component fetching `/api/posts`, which 404s, so the index perpetually showed a spinner/error and linked to nothing. That orphaned all 12 posts — no internal links, not in the sitemap, unreachable from crawl. Fixed: recovered the real copy into `app/blog/posts.ts`, added `app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` (SSG, per-post metadata + canonical + `BlogPosting` JSON-LD, and a CTA bridging that legacy inbox-management traffic into Lumina's product), and rebuilt `app/blog/page.tsx` as a server component that actually lists and links all 12. Also added missing meta descriptions and dropped stale "2024" references from titles.
3. **Sitemap/redirect gaps** — `sitemap.ts` had a `TODO` and never included blog posts; now generates all 12 post URLs with `lastModified`. Nav linked `/faq` → forced an unnecessary 301 hop to `/#faq`, which **didn't even exist as content** anywhere on the site (confirmed against the reference build too) — added a real FAQ section with `FAQPage` JSON-LD to the homepage and pointed the nav link straight at `/#faq`.

Validated via `npm run build && npm run start` on a scratch port: all 12 posts render (200), unknown slugs 404 correctly, sitemap lists 16 URLs, and head tags/JSON-LD all resolve to consistent Lumina branding.

One unrelated item worth flagging: `npm install` reported `next@14.2.5` has a known critical security vulnerability (patch available) — not an SEO issue, but worth a dependency bump separately.
